content:
9 min read
1 hour ago

--

The investigation started as another frontend bug. It ended with us questioning an assumption we’d been carrying for years. There wasn’t a production outage. No spike in error rates. No browser crashes. No failed deployments.
In fact, every monitoring dashboard suggested the release had gone exactly as planned. Yet within an hour of deployment, support tickets started appearing.
“Sometimes the Save button doesn’t respond.”
Not always.
Not for everyone.
Not on every browser.
The frustrating kind of bug. Refreshing the page usually fixed it. Trying again often worked. Users couldn’t reliably reproduce it. Neither could we. Those are the bugs that quietly consume engineering time — not because they’re catastrophic, but because they refuse to behave consistently enough to expose themselves.
Every Signal Pointed Somewhere Different
The first reports came from Chrome. Then Safari. A few days later, Edge users reported the same issue. Browser-specific? Apparently not.The API logs looked healthy. Every request that reached the backend completed successfully. Average latency hadn’t changed. CPU utilization was flat. Memory usage looked normal. Error rates remained close to zero. The backend had no evidence that anything was wrong. So naturally, we turned our attention to the frontend.
